message.content:
The air in Mr. Thorne’s shop was thick with the scent of old paper, cedar shavings, and the metallic tang of oil. Sunlight struggled to pierce the dust motes dancing above rows of ticking clocks—grandfather clocks standing like solemn sentinels, pocket watches glinting on velvet cushions, and cuckoo clocks hiding in shadowed corners.

Leo, no older than eight, stood on his tiptoes, pressing his nose against the glass case of a particularly ornate grandfather clock. Its pendulum swung with a hypnotic, rhythmic *tick-tock, tick-tock*.

"Mr. Thorne?" Leo whispered, not wanting to break the spell of the ticking.

The old man looked up from his workbench, where he was bent over a tiny mechanism with tweezers in one hand and a loupe over his right eye. He adjusted his spectacles and smiled, the skin around his eyes crinkling.

"Yes, Leo? You’ve been staring at that one for ten minutes. Is it speaking to you?"

Leo turned, his eyes wide. "No, but... it sounds like it’s counting something. What is it counting?"

Mr. Thorne set down his tweezers and wiped his hands on a rag. He gestured for Leo to come closer. "It’s counting heartbeats, little one. Or perhaps breaths. Or maybe just the moments we forget to notice."

Leo frowned, tilting his head. "But time isn’t real. Mom says time is just something we made up so we could go to school and eat dinner at the right time. She says it’s like a rule."

Mr. Thorne chuckled, a dry, rustling sound like pages turning. He picked up a small, disassembled pocket watch from the bench and held it out. "Come here.
